coredata.dc:title: Residual-dye-derived carbon dots from wastewater for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yueting
Dou, Bing
Zhao, Min
Shi, Zhen
Geng, Jialiang
Zhao, Zhihui
Xue, Zheng
Chen, Weichao
coredata.dc:description: With the development of resource recycling awareness, the number of research on the synthesis and utilization of carbon dots (CDs) from natural wastes continues to grow. Meanwhile, the little-studied synthesis of CDs originating from industrial waste has also become more relevant. In this work, reactive yellow 95 (Y95) molecules in exhausted dyeing wastewater were used as carbon source. By a simple one-step hydrothermal method, the CDs with stimuli-responsive fluorescent chromotropic properties were obtained. With the addition of hydrochloric acid to the CDs solution, the emission of the CDs was red-shifted from 470 nm to 514 nm, and the fluorescence changed from blue to green. The phenomenon was attributed to the synergistic effect of more CO defects on the surface of CDs and the protonation of the carbonyl groups after adding acid. Based on acid-induced fluorescent chromotropic performance, we designed anti-counterfeiting labels for textiles and a double-switching information encryption anti-counterfeiting ink. This work synthesized responsive fluorescent CDs using waste dyes and showed great potential in the field of resource recycling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dyeing wastewater
Stimulus-response
Anti-counterfeit
Resource recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs from Y95
Anti-counterfeiting and multilevel encryption applications of Y95-CDs

State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Shinan District, Qingdao (2022\u20133\u2013009-SW), the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT01).

This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Shinan District, Qingdao (2022-3-009-SW), the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT01).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure of Y95-CDs. a) TEM image of the Y95-CDs, inset: HR-TEM image of the Y95-CDs. b) Particle size distribution measured by TEM. c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the Y95-CDs. d) FT-IR spectra of Y95 and the Y95-CDs. e, f) High-resolution XPS C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of the Y95-CDs.

Absorption, emission properties, and TRPL of Y95-CDs. a) UV−vis absorption (red line), PL excitation (Ex) (λem = 470 nm, blue line), and emission (Em) (λex = 365 nm, green line) spectra of Y95-CDs. b) PL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of Y95-CDs. c) TRPL decay curves of the Y95-CDs.

Emission variation of Y95-CDs with different pH values. a) 3D fluorescence spectra of Y95-CDs at different pH values. b) Emission matrix of Y95-CDs under 365 nm excitation. c, d) Photographs of the Y95-CDs (left) and Y95-CDs in acid solution (right) under daylight (c) and 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (d), respectively.

Optical properties and structure of the G-CDs. a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Y95-CDs and G-CDs. b, c) High-resolution XPS C 1 s (b) and O 1 s (c) spectra of G-CDs. d) FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs. e) The PL emission spectra of G-CDs with different excitation wavelengths. f) The TRPL spectra of G-CDs.
Illustration of the Eg, HOMO, LUMO, and the mechanism of acid-induced emission redshift.

Y95-CDs security labeling on cotton fabrics. a, b, d, e) Photographs of the Y95-CDs@cotton-fabric and G-CDs@cotton-fabric under daylight (a, d) and UV light (b, e). c, f) fluorescence spectra of Y95-CDs@cotton-fabric and G-CDs@cotton-fabric.

Double encryption pattern of Y95-CDs on filter paper. a, b) Photographs of the Y95-CDs@paper and G-CDs@paper under daylight (a) and UV light (b), respectively. c, d) Photographs of acid drops in part of the patterns under daylight (c) and UV light (d), respectively. e) Photos of the encrypted messages under 365 nm. f) Photos of correct information under the dual action of acid and 365 nm excitation.
Fitted fluorescence lifetimes of Y95-CDs and G-CDs.

head.ce:title: Residual-dye-derived carbon dots from wastewater for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Manufacturing Low Carbon and Functionalized Textiles in the Universities of Shandong Province, College of Textiles & Clothing, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Manufacturing Low Carbon and Functionalized Textiles in the Universities of Shandong Province, College of Textiles & Clothing, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Manufacturing Low Carbon and Functionalized Textiles in the Universities of Shandong Province, College of Textiles & Clothing, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles, Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles, Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles, Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Qingdao University, 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the development of resource recycling awareness, the number of research on the synthesis and utilization of carbon dots (CDs) from natural wastes continues to grow. Meanwhile, the little-studied synthesis of CDs originating from industrial waste has also become more relevant. In this work, reactive yellow 95 (Y95) molecules in exhausted dyeing wastewater were used as carbon source. By a simple one-step hydrothermal method, the CDs with stimuli-responsive fluorescent chromotropic properties were obtained. With the addition of hydrochloric acid to the CDs solution, the emission of the CDs was red-shifted from 470 nm to 514 nm, and the fluorescence changed from blue to green. The phenomenon was attributed to the synergistic effect of more CO defects on the surface of CDs and the protonation of the carbonyl groups after adding acid. Based on acid-induced fluorescent chromotropic performance, we designed anti-counterfeiting labels for textiles and a double-switching information encryption anti-counterfeiting ink. This work synthesized responsive fluorescent CDs using waste dyes and showed great potential in the field of resource recycling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a newly developed carbon-based nanomaterial, have caused extensive research with excellent luminous stability, high biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and low cost. It can be prepared from carbon-containing compounds by controlling the experimental conditions. Dyes in dyeing wastewater containing conjugated structural carbons are expected to be precursors for the fabrication of CDs [1]. Therefore, the investigation of CDs fabricated from dyes is crucial for the recycling of dye wastewater, which also contributes to the achievement of carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals.

At present, waste can be value-added and reused by converting waste into CDs. High-performance functionalized CDs were synthesized from many wastes such as straw, coffee grounds [2,3], eggshells [4], pine cones [5], and discarded leather [6]. Whereas these studies were all based on biomass materials [7–9]. Unlike natural wastes, industrial wastes cause serious pollution to the environment. It is promising to apply this treatment technology to industrial waste. As it helps to shorten the process of waste elimination and reduce its accumulation in the environment [10–13]. For example, Kommula et al. used waste plastics to synthesize CDs for oxygen collection [14–16], providing an effective way to utilize waste plastics. However, there were fewer studies on industrial waste-derived CDs and more fields of industrial waste need to be studied. The synthesis of CDs derived from waste dyes is in need of research, dyeing wastewater, as one of the major pollutants of industry.

CDs are widely used in anti-counterfeiting applications due to their controllable fluorescent properties [17–19]. Stimulus-responsive fluorescence can provide more encrypted channels and increase the difficulty of counterfeiting, showing great potential for application [20–22]. As an available item in life, acid was chosen as the first choice for giving stimulus responses. However, most of the responses of CDs to acid stimulation are acid-induced enhancement or quenching, which limits their application in more scenarios. Therefore, it is necessary to develop acid-induced fluorescent chromotropic CDs. The reported acid-induced redshift of CDs is caused by protonation effects, but this theory does not apply to all phenomena. It is worthy of further study to enrich the theory of acid-induced redshift and provide a reference for future research.

In this work, the CDs based on reactive yellow 95 (Y95-CDs) were prepared with stimuli-responsive fluorescent chromotropic properties by a green and simple one-pot hydrothermal route. The effect of salinity and alkali in wastewater on CDs synthesis was also investigated. Interestingly, the Y95-CDs exhibited the property of acid-responsive fluorescence. With the addition of acid, the emission of Y95-CDs redshifted from 470 nm to 514 nm, and the fluorescence changed from blue to green. Based on the Stimuli-responsive chromotropic properties, we prepared Y95-CDs as an anticounterfeit ink for anti-counterfeiting trademarks of clothing and two-switch-mode information encryption. This work investigated the effect of dye molecular structure and properties on the formation of CDs, which realized the upgrading and reuse of residual dyes, and provided a new idea for the reuse of dyeing wastewater.

NaCl, Na2CO3, and HCl were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. C.I. reactive yellow 95 was supplied by Ever light Chemistry Co., Ltd., (Taiwan, China). 100% cotton fabric was provided by Lufeng weaving & dyeing Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). White vinegar for kitchen use can be purchased at a nearby supermarket. Commercial anti-counterfeiting marker pen purchased from Kearing Flagship Store. The water used in this work is ultrapure water obtained from Direct-Q®·8ultra pure water system (Millipore, USA) with a conductivity of 0.9 μS/cm.

The dyeing process is shown in Figure S2(a) (Supplementary). The cotton fabric was removed and the remaining liquid was the initial wastewater. To reduce the cost of wastewater treatment, we only use a filter head of 0.22 μm to remove obvious impurities, and the wastewater is collected without further separation and purification. The concentration of dye in the wastewater was calculated according to the formula (1) and subsequently the waste liquid was concentrated to a dye concentration of 4 g/L and stored for later use.
(1)
A = Kbc
In this formula, A is the absorbance, K is the molar absorption coefficient, b is the cuvette length, and c is the concentration of the substance.

The pre-prepared wastewater was added to the reactor and kept in an oven at 200°C for 6 hours. After the reaction, Y95-CDs were obtained by dialyzing in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 12 hours, and the solid powder of Y95-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying.

An excess of hydrochloric acid was added to the Y95-CDs solution, and subsequently, Y95-CDs were obtained by dialyzing in a 500 Da dialysis bag. Freeze-drying and stored for subsequent characterization. Notably, purified CDs were used for characterization. Considering the reduction of treatment cost and practical promotion, CDs without purification are used in the application part.

The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was tested by the U-3900 H ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves were recorded from a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Britain). Fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were calculated using quinine sulfate as a standard reference, as detailed in the supporting literature. The morphology and particle size of the Y95-CDs were statistically and analytically obtained by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HR-TEM spectrometer (JEOL JEM-2100 Plus), respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected using a Rigaku Ultima IV X diffractometer (Bruker Co., Ltd., German). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS system off Thermo Scientific (USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet iN10 FT-IR spectrometer. Cyclic voltammetry tests were measured on the CHI660E B21525 electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai, China) using a three-electrode system, as detailed in the Supplementary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs from Y95
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs based on Y95 were easily prepared using Y95 solution through one-pot hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for six hours. The morphology of Y95-CDs was observed by TEM and shown in Fig. 1(a) [23–25]. The size of CDs ranges between 2.5 and 4.5 nm with an average diameter of 3 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The HR-TEM image inset in Fig. 1(a) presents the lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite. CDs exhibit an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm, as indicated by the peak of XRD patterns at approximately 25° (Fig. 1(c)) [26–28]. The formation of graphite-like core structures indicates that Y95 dye molecules can be successfully grown into CDs by hydrothermal carbonization at 200 °C.

The chemical structures of the Y95-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the surface of the Y95-CDs is rich in hydroxyl groups, according to the broad peaks of 3000–3600 cm−1, while CC, CO, and C-O stretching vibrations present at 1400–1500, 1710, and 1100 cm−1, respectively [29,30]. The surface functional groups of the Y95-CDs were further investigated by XPS, as shown in Fig. 1(e, f). The high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrum confirms the presence of CC/C-C (284.81 eV), C-O (286.1 eV), and CO (288.8 eV). The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum of the CDs confirms the presence of CO (532.1 eV), and O-H/C-O (533.5 eV) [26]. The abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface make the Y95-CDs have good hydrophilicity [18].

The UV–visible absorption, fluorescence spectra, and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves of the aqueous solutions of Y95-CDs were measured. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits two apparent peaks at 244 and 295 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transitions of the CC in the core of the Y95-CDs and n-π* transitions of the CO on the surface states respectively ( Fig. 2(a)) [31,32]. Y95-CDs have optimum fluorescence emission at 480 nm under excitation wavelengths of 380 nm, while showing an excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 2(b)). The TRPL curves and fitted curves of Y95-CDs are shown in Fig. 2(c) [33]. The fitting curve is fitted by two components, corresponding to carbon core intrinsic states excitation (π-π*) and defect state excitation (n-π*) on the surface respectively, which is consistent with the results of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy [34,35]. Y95-CDs show conventional carbon core luminescence with an average lifetime of 4.7213 ns at 470 nm. The fluorescence QYs of the Y95-CDs is 9.2%.

Subsequently, we further investigated the acid response properties of Y95-CDs. The emission center of Y95-CDs undergoes an obvious red shift with the drop of hydrochloric acid, as shown in Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 3(a)), which is different from the previously reported acid quenching or acid-enhanced emission properties [29,36–38]. Under 365 nm excitation, Y95-CDs underwent a 44 nm redshift from 470 nm to 514 nm when the pH dropped to 3.2 (Fig. 3(b)). In order to study the cause of emission red shift, G-CDs were obtained by dialyzing to neutrality after the addition of excess hydrochloric acid to the Y95-CDs solution. The photos of Y95-CDs and G-CDs in daylight and irradiated by 365 nm are shown in Fig. 3(c, d), the aqueous solution of Y95-CDs changed from nearly colorless to a pale yellow color, and the luminescence of CDs changes from blue to green.

In order to clarify the mechanism of acid-induced luminescence redshift, G-CDs were dialyzed to neutrality and then used for characterization. The UV–vis curve of G-CDs exhibits two peaks at the same position as the Y95-CDs ( Fig. 4(a)). It is different that the absorption of G-CDs is significantly enhanced at 295 nm, which reveals the n-π* transition on the surface of G-CDs is enhanced. Meanwhile, the absorption curve of G-CDs extends to the visible region compared to Y95-CDs. The absorption tail comes mainly from CO-related functional groups, and the enhancement of the absorption tail suggests that G-CDs contain more CO groups than Y95-CDs [39,40]. The enhanced absorption of G-CDs in the visible region (inset in Fig. 4(a)) explains the yellowing of the solution. By analyzing the C 1 s and O 1 s spectrum of high-resolution XPS shown in Fig. 4(b, c), G-CDs present the same varieties of elements and functional groups as Y95-CDs. However, the CO bond in the G-CDs is significantly increased according to the functional group content in Table 1, and the atomic ratio of O and C increased from 0.10 to 0.19, revealing a higher degree of surface oxidation of G-CDs [41]. Higher levels of oxidation produce more defects on the surface, which is the cause of its luminous redshift [42,43]. The FT-IR analysis further demonstrates that the stretching vibrations of CO at 1710 cm−1 are enhanced (Fig. 4(d)). The enhanced hydrogen bonding absorption peaks of G-CDs at 2300–2700 cm−1 may be attributed to the formation of more intramolecular hydrogen bonds by the increased CO on the surface of G-CDs. The G-CDs have an emission of 507 nm under 365 nm excitation, while the excitation dependence becomes weaker compared to Y95-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4(e). And the G-CDs have a higher fluorescence QY of 16.8%.

To further analyze the mechanism of acid-induced emission redshift, the TRPL decay curve of G-CDs was analyzed, as shown in Fig. 4(f). The fitted curve of G-CDs was fitted by two components similar to Y95-CDs ( Table 2). A significant change appears in the Weight of the two luminescent centers. This means that both Y95-CDs and G-CDs have two identical emission sources, the addition of acid only affects the surface state and not the carbon core luminescence of the CDs. The fast component τ1 decreased from 61.89% to 16.69% and the slow component τ2 increased from 38.11% to 83.31%. The wavelength of π-π* recombination emission is longer, and the increased weight of τ2 makes the recombination emission of G-CDs red-shift, and with a longer average fluorescence lifetime in the G-CDs [31,44]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the carbon core emission of π-π* recombination is the main luminescence center of Y95-CDs. After the acid is added, the n-π* recombination of surface defect state dominates the luminescence of G-CDs [32]. Moreover, the emission wavelength of the dialyzed G-CDs is 507 nm, which is lower than the non-dialyzed one (514 nm) (Fig. 4(f)). This is due to a further emission redshift of the G-CDs by the protonation of CO in non-dialyzed hydrochloric acid [43]. Electronegative CO has a strong electron-accepting property, further reducing the LUMO energy level of the CDs [30].

The optical energy band gap (Eg) of Y95-CDs and G-CDs further illustrates the mechanism of luminescence change. Compared to Y95-CDs, G-CDs have lower lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level and narrower optical band gap according to Table S1. According to the previous structural analysis, both types of CDs have conjugated carbon and bonded oxygen atoms on their surfaces, whereas G-CDs have more CO on their surfaces. The CO reduces the LUMO energy level of the Y95-CDs. This may be due to the local distortion of the carbon structure caused by sp2 hybridized carbon [45]. The lowering of the LUMO energy level leads to a narrowing of the optical bandgap, which results in a luminescence redshift.

Based on the results, the mechanism of acid-induced emission redshift of the Y95-CDs is proposed, as shown in Fig. 5. When Y95-CDs are irradiated, the carbon core is the main blue light emitter. With the addition of hydrochloric acid, the surface defects of the CDs increase and dominate the luminescence. The appearance of CO reduces the LUMO energy level of CDs, resulting in a narrower band gap and red-shifted luminescence [46–49]. Hence, the luminescence of CDs from blue to green.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-counterfeiting and multilevel encryption applications of Y95-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeit products of many popular brands flooded the market. Except judging the authenticity by subtle differences of workmanship, it is needed for non-professional consumers to identify through more intuitive anti-counterfeiting labels. Considering the reduction of treatment cost and practical promotion, CDs without purification are used in the part. Y95-CDs were designed as an anti-counterfeiting label on cotton fabrics (Y95-CDs@cotton-fabric). One drop of Y95-CDs solution was dripped onto the cotton fabric. Fig. 6(a, b) shows the circular mark of Y95-CDs. The mark is completely invisible in daylight without any effect on the appearance of the fabric. Under the 365 nm irradiation, the mark shows blue fluorescence. Subsequently, a drop of white kitchen vinegar was added to the mark. As shown in Fig. 6(d, e), the mark changed to yellow color in daylight, and the emission was redshifted to green color under an excitation of 365 nm. Fig. 6(c, f) shows the fluorescence spectra of the mark before and after acid addition, respectively. The red shift of the markers with drops of acetic acid is smaller than that of G-CDs, due to that acetic acid is less acidic than hydrochloric acid, and the markers are in a less acidic environment. The stimulus-response performance of Y95-CDs with the white vinegar makes it convenient to use the anti-counterfeiting label in daily life. Moreover, the yellowed security marks can be washed clean without affecting the beauty of the clothing, and can be used as a label for new clothes to avoid washing and secondary sales.

In addition, Y95-CDs were used as the ink for multilevel information encryption. Fig. 7 shows the marks written on filter paper using a marker pen loaded with Y95-CDs solution (Y95-CDs@paper). The treated filter paper has no obvious changes in daylight, and has blue emission under ultraviolet lamp excitation (Fig. 7(a, b)). Then, a few drops of acetic acid were added to part of the pattern, and then acid-treated parts were yellowish and visible to the naked eye, with green emission under ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 7(c, d)). Subsequently, the number “145” was written down with a Y95-CDs marker pen. Add a few strokes to the number “145” using a commercial security marker to turn “145” into “386” (Fig. 7(e, f)). Under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, the labeled “386” showed blue fluorescence (Fig. 7(e)). After adding a few drops of acid to the mark, the fluorescence of the commercial fluorescent agent was quenched and the Y95-CDs turned from blue to green, revealing the encrypted numbers “145” (Fig. 7(f)). Utilizing the property of fluorescence color change in response to acid stimulation, Y95-CDs fluorescent notation has blue emission under UV lamp irradiation, and the correct pattern is revealed only when acid is added, realizing double-switching information encryption.

In this work, we explored the synthesis of CDs from Y95 exhaust-dye wastewater. The Y95-CDs exhibited acid-induced fluorescent chromotropic performance. With the addition of acid, the emission of the CDs redshifted from 470 nm to 514 nm under 365 nm excitation. This red shift phenomenon was due to the synergistic effect of more defects arising on the surface of Y95-CDs and the protonation of the carbonyl group in the acidic environment. Based on the acid-responsive fluorescence, with the addition of white vinegar, Y95-CDs were used as a daily anti-counterfeiting mark for fabrics and also a label for new clothing to avoid secondary sales. In addition, in conjunction with a commercial blue fluorescent marker, a novel information encryption code was designed. This work puts forward a new idea for the reuse of dyeing wastewater and the value-added utilization of waste dyes.

Zhihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Zhen Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Bing Dou: Software, Methodology. Yueting Cao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zheng Xue: Methodology. Min Zhao: Methodology. Jialiang Geng: Methodology. Weichao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Shinan District, Qingdao (2022–3–009-SW), the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133822.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance inkjet printing ink: properties and application effects of vinyl sulfone reactive dye-based inks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, C. Cao, F. Feng, K. Fang, M. Wang, R. Xie, Z. Zhao, W. Chen, High-performance inkjet printing ink: Properties and application effects of vinyl sulfone reactive dye-based inks, Journal of Molecular Liquids, 369 (2023) 120864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots from spent coffee grounds via ball-milling: application in fluorescent chemosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Jeong, C.H. Park, D. Yi, H. Yang, Green synthesis of carbon dots from spent coffee grounds via ball-milling: Application in fluorescent chemosensors, Journal of Cleaner Production, 392 (2023) 136250.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from coffee grounds for bioimaging and sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, W. Li, B. Wu, Z. Li, S. Wang, Y. Liu, D. Pan, M. Wu, Facile synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from coffee grounds for bioimaging and sensing, Chemical Engineering Journal, 300 (2016) 75-82.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biosynthesis of the narrowband deep-red emissive carbon nanodots from eggshells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Deng, C. Shen, W. Zhao, G. Zheng, F. Jiao, Q. Lou, K. Liu, C.-X. Shan, L. Dong, Biosynthesis of the Narrowband Deep-Red Emissive Carbon Nanodots from Eggshells, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 11 (2023) 6535-6544.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-step and green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots and carbon nanofibers from pine Fruit

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Shahba, M. Sabet, Two-Step and Green Synthesis of Highly Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots and Carbon Nanofibers from Pine Fruit, J Fluoresc, 30 (2020) 927-938.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turning waste into treasure: multicolor carbon dots synthesized from waste leather scrap and their application in anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, L. Li, M. Yan, J. Ma, J. Wang, C. Liu, Y. Bao, H. Jin, Q. Fan, Turning Waste into Treasure: Multicolor Carbon Dots Synthesized from Waste Leather Scrap and their Application in Anti-Counterfeiting, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 11 (2023) 5082-5092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessing the biomass-based carbon dots and their composites for photocatalytic treatment of wastewater

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Serial nanomaterial synthesis from Ilex paraguariensis biomass for efficient Cr6+ adsorption and detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.F. Mercado, M. Paredes-Laverde, S. Céspedes, R.A. Torres-Palma, Serial nanomaterial synthesis from Ilex paraguariensis biomass for efficient Cr6+ adsorption and detection, Journal of Cleaner Production, 401 (2023) 136681.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-narrow-bandwidth deep-red electroluminescence based on green plant-derived carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Chen, Z. Wang, T. Pang, Q. Teng, C. Li, N. Jiang, S. Zheng, R. Zhang, Y. Zheng, D. Chen, F. Yuan, Ultra-Narrow-Bandwidth Deep-Red Electroluminescence Based on Green Plant-Derived Carbon Dots, Adv Mater, 35 (2023) e2302275.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Upcycling: converting waste plastics into paramagnetic, conducting, solid, pure carbon microspheres

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of boron-doped carbon dots from Chinese herbal residues for Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photodegradation applications
